channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Knicks in ’73: Revisiting the Charts From a Championship Year
item.description: Listen to tracks from War, Vicki Lawrence and other artists who had hits in May 1973.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: ABC Photo Archives/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Tony Orlando and Dawn.

item.title: The Victoria and Albert Museum Gets Nostalgic for Britain’s Lost Music Venues
item.description: A new exhibition charts the cyclical forces destroying nightlife spots, even as independent spaces fight developers, complaining neighbors and shifting social habits.
item.dc:creator: Jillian Rayfield
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The band Floral Image performing in London at the Moth Club, which says it is threatened by a neighboring building development.

item.title: Carter Brey, New York Philharmonic Cellist, Retires
item.description: Carter Brey, the New York Philharmonic’s principal cellist for the last 30 years, is retiring from the orchestra at the end of this season.
item.dc:creator: Anastasia Tsioulcas and James Estrin
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: During recent bows, Brey acknowledged the audience’s standing ovation then lithely maneuvered himself out of the spotlight.

item.title: ‘Earth, Wind & Fire’ Review: Questlove’s Tribute to an Inspiration
item.description: In “To Be Celestial vs. That’s the Weight of the World,” he draws a compelling portrait of Maurice White, the band’s founder, and his enduring legacy.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny
item.category: Earth, Wind & Fire: To Be Celestial vs That's the Weight of the World (Movie)

item.title: The Gustavo Dudamel Era in Los Angeles Approaches Its End
item.description: Gustavo Dudamel’s last concerts at Walt Disney Concert Hall as the Los Angeles Philharmonic’s maestro were a symbol of his impact after 17 years.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Jake Michaels for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, whose tenure as the music and artistic director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic concludes this summer.

item.title: Sting Never Gave Up on His Musical ‘The Last Ship.’ Now It’s Back
item.description: After his 2014 musical failed on Broadway, the musician is bringing a revised version of it to the Metropolitan Opera for a limited run this week.
item.dc:creator: Alan Light
item.media:credit: Pete Kiehart for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I loved it,” Sting said of his musical “The Last Ship.” “I’m sad it didn’t go on longer.”

item.title: What Happens When ‘Star Wars’ Replaces Mozart?
item.description: Across the United States, orchestras are programming more live performances of movie soundtracks in a bid for box office revenue.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.category: Wolf Trap National Park for the Performing Arts (Fairfax County, Va)
item.media:credit: Nate Smallwood for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Star Wars” fans at Heinz Hall in Pittsburgh, where the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra recently performed a live soundtrack for “The Empire Strikes Back.”

item.title: Patrick Bruel, French Singer, Is Taken Into Custody Over Sex Assault Claims
item.description: Mr. Bruel, long a beloved celebrity in France, has denied allegations raised by 13 women dating to 1997.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Alain Jocard/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The French singer and actor Patrick Bruel in Paris last year.

item.title: The New York Hip-Hop Soundtracking the Knicks’ Run to the N.B.A. Finals
item.description: From old-school stars to young upstarts, the birthplace of hip-hop is basking in the big moment.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Jamie Squire/Getty Images
item.media:description: The rapper Fat Joe has been one of the most visible supporters of the Knicks on their playoff run.

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Talks About His Place in American Music
item.description: As he opens an expansive cultural center not far from the Jersey Shore boardwalk, the singer describes himself as “a small link in a big chain.”
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.category: Springsteen, Bruce, Archives and Center for American Music
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bruce Springsteen outside the new Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music.

item.title: Barry Manilow Can’t Smile Without Fans’ Help on This Song
item.description: The singer has bigger hits but “Can’t Smile Without You” has become a personal touchstone for fans who have helped him perform it live.
item.dc:creator: Rob Tannenbaum
item.media:credit: Leon Bennett/Getty Images
item.media:description: Manilow, 82, is slated to resume his “farewell tour” later this month after postponing dates amid a bout with lung cancer.

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Opens His Music Center
item.description: Jon Bon Jovi, Public Enemy and others joined him to ring in the Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music, a $50 million archive and cultural hub.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.category: Springsteen, Bruce, Archives and Center for American Music
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bruce Springsteen greets Jon Bon Jovi at a ceremony for the Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music in Long Branch, N.J., on Saturday.

item.title: Despite Protest, Ye Takes the Stage for Thousands of European Fans
item.description: Nearly 40,000 fans came out to hear the rapper formerly known as Kanye West in the Netherlands on Saturday, even as other European countries had canceled his concerts.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Mouneb Taim/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Mouneb Taim
item.media:description: “This crowd is too crazy,” Ye said inside the GelreDome stadium in Arnhem, the Netherlands, on Saturday.

item.title: Kennedy Center Loses Case Against Musician Who Canceled Over Trump Naming Dispute
item.description: A judge sided with jazz performer Chuck Redd, who canceled a 2025 holiday concert after President Trump’s name was added to the building.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A judge threw out a lawsuit filed by the John F. Kennedy Center against a jazz musician who canceled a Christmas Eve performance.

item.title: Madonna Premieres ‘Confessions II,’ a Star-Studded Short Film, at Tribeca Film Festival
item.description: “Confessions II” features songs from the pop star’s coming album of the same name. At one point, green lasers shoot from the pelvises of gyrating women.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
item.media:description: Madonna during a surprise concert in Times Square on Thursday. “I came to this earth to be a doer, not a watcher,” she said after her new film premiered on Friday.

item.title: Taylor Swift, Madonna: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Taylor Swift is back with a “Toy Story” contribution while Madonna returns to the dance floor.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
item.media:description: Madonna performed new songs, including “Love Sensation,” at a surprise concert in Times Square in New York on Thursday.

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards: What to Expect
item.description: This year’s annual celebration of the best on Broadway is being hosted by Pink.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The CBS telecast of the Tonys introduces the latest plays and musicals to a television audience of several million.

item.title: Speight Jenkins, Who Brought Adventure to Seattle Opera, Dies at 89
item.description: He had never produced a performance when he was hired as the company’s general director in 1983. But over 31 years (and lots of Wagner) he became beloved.
item.dc:creator: Tim Page

item.title: Met Museum’s ‘Musical Bodies’ Blurs Humans and Instruments
item.description: “Musical Bodies,” a new exhibition at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, revels in the blurry boundaries between humans and instruments.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Ye Fan
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Says Lee Greenwood and Christopher Macchio Will Replace Freedom 250 Artists
item.description: The confusion surrounding the concerts planned for the National Mall highlights the partisan tensions around national celebrations of America’s 250th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Andrew Kelly/Ap Photo/Andrew Kelly
item.media:description: Lee Greenwood performing last month. In his announcement, President Trump called Mr. Greenwood’s song “God Bless the U.S.A.” “one of the greatest hits of all time.”

item.title: Does the World Still Misunderstand M.I.A.?
item.description: A winding interview with the pop globalist about her legacy of art and incitement.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Review: A Musical That’s a Little Too Tranquilized
item.description: While the 1999 movie went for melodrama, this stage adaptation with songs by Aimee Mann honors the memoir’s coolly clinical prose.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The cast of “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan, from left: Mia Pak, Sally Shaw, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, Ta’Rea Campbell and King Princess.

item.title: Kennedy Center Tells Staff to Immediately Remove Trump’s Name From Documents
item.description: The center’s general counsel also said that a federal judge’s order meant the president’s name must be taken off outdoor and indoor signage by June 12.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Mandel Ngan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Parking signage bearing the “Trump Kennedy Center” branding must be altered by June 12, according to an internal memo sent to the institution’s staff, while other documents must be changed immediately.

item.title: Limmie Pulliam, Tenor Who Overcame the Stigma of His Size, Dies at 50
item.description: He quit singing in his early 20s when opera companies objected to his appearance, but after a 12-year absence he revived his career to acclaim.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Pommelien Thijs, a Pop Star for Half a Country
item.description: Pommelien Thijs’s catchy tunes are beloved in Belgium — in the Dutch-speaking part, at least.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Ilvy Njiokiktjien
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times

item.title: A Chance to Shop the Paris Opera’s Jewel-Encrusted Closet
item.description: Vintage ball gowns, potbellied fat suits and diaphanous tunics were among the offerings when the Paris Opera held a public sale of opera and ballet costumes.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Julien Mignot for The New York Times
item.media:description: Avalon Alexander traveled to Paris from Malta for the costume sale.

item.title: John McClain, Who Helped Keep Michael Jackson’s Profits Flowing, Dies at 72
item.description: A music executive with longstanding ties to the Jackson family, he worked to make Janet a star and Michael a posthumous commercial powerhouse.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Wally Skalij/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John McClain in 1998. He was a producer of the Jackson biopic “Michael,” released in April, which has earned about $850 million worldwide.

item.title: What’s Next Now That Live Nation Has Been Found to Act as a Monopoly
item.description: The music industry may be significantly changed by the judicial rulings to come, but there is still a lot of lawyering left to do.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Now that a federal jury has found that Live Nation acted as a monopoly, there will likely be months of legal wrangling to determine what damages it will pay or whether the verdict will stand.

item.title: Even on His Own, Nick Is Still a Jonas Brother
item.description: With a starring role in “Power Ballad” and ambitions across mediums, the 33-year-old is carving a singular path.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer and OK McCausland

item.title: N.B.A. Finals Introduce Music by Nicholas Britell and Nas
item.description: The league is rolling out music by the composer Nicholas Britell and the rapper Nas that, if successful, could become the sport’s sonic shorthand.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sonic dream team: Nas, left, and Nicholas Britell in Brooklyn.

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Charlie Parker
item.description: Bird helped usher in bebop and transformed the alto saxophone into an instrument of startling possibility. Here’s an 11-song primer on his enduring legacy.
item.dc:creator: Marcus J. Moore
